title: The swap
steps.0.text: Swap the two numbers. Afterwards `morning` should be 12 and `evening` should be 8. The numbers 8 and 12 appear only where the names are created.
steps.1.text: Swap the two shift names the same way. `morningStaff` becomes Boris, `eveningStaff` becomes Ana.
why.empty: script.js is empty. You deleted everything instead of fixing it.
why.untouched: script.js is exactly as it was.
hint2.untouched: The console shows two identical numbers. They were supposed to be swapped, not identical.
why.crashed: Your code stopped with an error.
hint2.crashed: Look at the Console panel on the right.
why.typed-answer: You typed the value by hand instead of swapping it.
hint2.typed-answer: Each of the four values appears only once — where its name is created.
hint3.typed-answer: Tomorrow the shifts will be 40 and 55, and the people will be different. If you typed the answer by hand, your code will lie. The swap has to work with any values.
hint2.morning-wrong: The first line of the swap destroys the old value of `morning`. It has to be saved before that.
hint3.morning-wrong: Think of two cups with different liquids. You cannot swap them by pouring directly. You need a third, empty one.
hint4.morning-wrong: Create a third name, give it the value of `morning`, then point `morning` at `evening`, and finally take back what you saved.
hint2.evening-wrong: The eight has disappeared somewhere along the way. Check which line overwrote it.
why.staff-wrong: The shift names are not swapped.
hint2.staff-wrong: The same three lines, in the same order. It works the same for text as for a number.
hint3.staff-wrong: The second saved name is separate from the first. One name cannot hold two things at once.
why.wrong-count: There are not four lines in the console.
blocks.1.text: The morning shift sold 8 cups, the evening shift 12. It turns out the numbers were swapped and have to change places. The code does exactly that. Or so it looks:
blocks.3.text: Two identical numbers. Nothing crashed, nothing is underlined in red. The result is simply meaningless.
blocks.5.text: The first line says: `morning` now points at whatever `evening` points at. That is, at 12.
blocks.6.text: And at that moment the eight is already gone. Nothing is holding it. Nothing points at it.
blocks.7.text: The second line says: let `evening` point at whatever `morning` points at. And `morning` already points at 12. So both become 12.
blocks.9.text: The equals sign in maths says the two sides are the same thing. In code it says something else: take the right-hand side and make the left-hand one point at it.
blocks.11.text: Here `a` becomes 2. But `b` does not become 1. It goes one way only — the left changes, the right stays put.
blocks.13.text: Since the first assignment eats the old value, it has to be saved somewhere before that:
blocks.15.text: Three lines, in exactly this order. The first puts it aside. The second overwrites — now it is safe. The third gives back what was saved. The saved name is created once and never changes, which is why it is `const`.
blocks.17.text: Numbers, text, anything — the three lines are the same. There is also a trick that swaps numbers without a third name: `a = a + b`, then `b = a - b`, then `a = a - b`. Do not memorize it — it is here only to show that the third name is not laziness. The trick works with numbers and breaks with everything else:
blocks.19.text: With text the plus joins, and there is no subtraction to take it apart again. The trick works for whole numbers and breaks everywhere else. The third name has no such limit.
blocks.21.text: Until now the lines in your code could be shuffled without consequence. From here on they cannot.
blocks.22.text: Every line changes the state, and the next one works on the changed version. That is also why code is hard to read — to understand line 40 you need to know what the first 39 did.
blocks.24.text: You will see this form too. It does the same thing:
blocks.26.text: It looks like magic and it is not. On the right a temporary list is built in reversed order, on the left it is handed back out into the two names. The temporary thing still exists — the language just makes it for you and throws it away at once. It arrives in section fourteen, together with what those square brackets are.
blocks.27.quote: The computer does not swap. It overwrites. A swap is something you build out of three overwrites — and their order is not a matter of taste.
blocks.29.items.0: Assignment goes one way: the left starts pointing at the right.
blocks.29.items.1: The first overwrite eats the old value. That is where the third name comes from.
blocks.29.items.2: Three lines, in exactly this order: save, overwrite, give back.
blocks.29.items.3: It works with any value. The arithmetic trick works only with whole numbers.
blocks.29.items.4: From here on the order of the lines matters.
walkthrough.0.text: The first four lines are not touched. That is where the names are created and the only place the values are written. That is why four of the checks count: type 12 a second time and you have written the answer rather than worked it out.
walkthrough.1.text: `const keepCups = morning;` — this is the line that was missing. It does nothing visible, which is exactly what makes it easy to skip. Without it the next line erases the eight for good.
walkthrough.2.text: `morning = evening;` is the same line that was in the broken version. It is safe now — not because it changed, but because something before it holds the old value.
walkthrough.3.text: `evening = keepCups;` gives back what was saved. This is where you see why the three lines cannot be reordered: each one depends on the previous having already run.
walkthrough.4.text: The second swap is literally the same, with a second saved name. `keepStaff` is separate from `keepCups`, because one name cannot hold two things at once. The values are text rather than numbers — the three lines do not care.
walkthrough.5.text: The saved names are `const`, because they are created once and never change. `morning` and `evening` are `let`, because those are the ones being reassigned — that is the point of the lesson. Here `let` and `const` are not a matter of taste: each one tells the reader which values move and which do not.
